paper_title: Federated Deep Reinforcement Learning for Open RAN Slicing in 6G Networks
paper_claims: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.

source_questions.claim: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which complete statement accurately reflects the approach and outcomes: federated deep reinforcement learning where MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, yielding privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC? [A] MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, producing privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions and enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. [B] Centralized training at the near-RT RIC with raw data sharing and unweighted aggregation yields policies that are not privacy-preserving, maintain only eMBB throughput SLAs, and require high-latency inference at the non-RT RIC. [C] MVNOs locally train A2C policies and send gradients to a centralized unit, achieving generalization only under i.i.d data, failing URLLC delay SLAs, and performing inference on user equipment. [D] MVNOs locally train DDPG policies but aggregate at the near-RT RIC, producing non–privacy-preserving policies that maintain URLLC SLAs only under homogeneous data and require inference at the core network.

source_questions.claim: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In the described setup, what combination of training, aggregation, policy properties, SLA guarantees, data heterogeneity, and inference correctly characterizes the outcome? [A] Local DDPG training with weighted aggregation at the near-RT RIC yields privacy-preserving but not generalizable policies that maintain only eMBB throughput SLAs under i.i.d conditions and use high-latency inference at the non-RT RIC. [B] Centralized PPO training with raw data sharing yields policies that violate URLLC delay SLAs and rely on offline, high-latency inference at the near-RT RIC. [C] MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, producing privacy-preserving, generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. [D] Federated supervised learning with gradient averaging at the core network generalizes only under i.i.d data, fails to maintain SLAs, and performs inference at a far-RT controller.

source_questions.claim: Federated deep reinforcement learning, in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC, produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC.

source_questions.question: Assertion (A): Federated deep reinforcement learning in which MVNOs locally train DDPG-based bandwidth allocation policies and share model updates for weighted aggregation at a non-RT RIC produces privacy-preserving and generalizable RAN slicing policies that maintain URLLC delay and eMBB throughput SLAs under heterogeneous non-i.i.d conditions while enabling low-latency online inference at the near-RT RIC. source_questions.answer: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

paper_title: Web-Based Volunteer Distributed Computing for Handling Time-Critical Urgent Workloads
paper_claims: Browser-based volunteer distributed computing using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms.

In web-based volunteer computing, a small minority of long dwell time sessions complete most tasks while many short sessions complete none, making performance dependent on value sessions and imposing overhead from non-value sessions.

Checkpointing partial results, batching tasks, and asynchronously prefetching new tasks increase the amount of useful work completed during volatile browsing sessions at the cost of higher server-side load.
Tuning task granularity is essential because smaller tasks increase the likelihood of value sessions but also amplify task management and communication overheads.

For data-bound workloads, XMLHttpRequest communication can outperform WebSockets, whereas compute-bound kernels are relatively insensitive to protocol choice and their browser-executed performance relative to native code depends on algorithm characteristics and browser engine optimizations.

source_questions.claim: Browser-based volunteer distributed computing using WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately describes the viability and requirements of using browsers for volunteer distributed computing to handle urgent tasks? [A] Browser-based volunteer distributed computing that uses WebWorkers and a centralized cross-origin task manager can viably execute embarrassingly parallel urgent workloads without requiring any client-side software installation across heterogeneous platforms. [B] Browser-based volunteer distributed computing can only execute non-urgent workloads and requires client-side software installation on each device, even when WebWorkers are used. [C] Such computing is viable only on homogeneous platforms with native agents, regardless of whether tasks are embarrassingly parallel. [D] Viability is limited to shared-memory workloads coordinated by a same-origin manager and requires browser extensions.

[B] When it uses Service Workers and offline caching with a decentralized task manager. [C] When it employs WebWorkers in conjunction with a centralized cross-origin task manager so tasks run in the browser across heterogeneous platforms without client-side installation. [D] Only when devices share identical hardware and a native client is preinstalled.

paper_title: A topological analysis of cointegrated data: a Z24 Bridge case study
paper_claims: Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.

The nonlinear dependence of the second natural frequency on temperature leads linear cointegration to leave residual topological structure, while Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration trained on data that include the nonlinear regime more effectively removes these effects.

Persistent homology comparisons using Wasserstein distances on Vietoris-Rips complexes of embedded series provide a quantitative topology-based metric for assessing the effectiveness of cointegration in detrending environmental and operational variability.

source_questions.claim: Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): For Z24 Bridge natural frequency data, time-delay embeddings of the raw time series and of cointegrated residuals show which combination of topologies and drivers? [A] Raw embeddings are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects, whereas residual embeddings are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes. [B] Raw embeddings are open-ball and characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes, whereas residual embeddings are toroidal due to cyclic temperature effects. [C] Both raw and residual embeddings are toroidal due to seasonal forcing. [D] Both raw and residual embeddings are open-ball due to de-trended white-noise fluctuations.

source_questions.claim: Time-delay embeddings of Z24 Bridge natural frequency time series exhibit a toroidal topology driven by cyclic temperature effects, whereas embeddings of cointegrated residuals exhibit an open-ball topology characteristic of approximately Gaussian white-noise processes.

[A] Raw series form a spherical shell driven by diurnal temperature cycles, while cointegrated residuals form a torus associated with colored noise. [B] Raw series form a torus induced by cyclic temperature, while cointegrated residuals form an open ball consistent with approximately Gaussian white noise. [C] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form open balls, but only the residuals are approximately Gaussian. [D] Raw series and cointegrated residuals both form tori, reflecting persistent periodicity in both.

[C] Linear cointegration and Gaussian process-based nonlinear cointegration perform equivalently regardless of training data coverage of the nonlinear regime. [D] Residual topology arises only if the first natural frequency is nonlinear in temperature, making method choice irrelevant.

[D] Residual topology is independent of temperature dependence, so both methods behave identically regardless of training data.

paper_title: ADAS: A Simple Active-and-Adaptive Baseline for Cross-Domain 3D Semantic Segmentation
paper_claims: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.

source_questions.claim: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description fully captures an approach for unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation that uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data? [A] It uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to create cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings, and it enhances existing UDA methods via targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data. [B] It relies on asymmetrical 3D-only features without cross-modal interaction, does not train a domain discriminator or perform active selection, works only in unsupervised settings, and pseudo-labels all target data indiscriminately. [C] It uses symmetrical 2D–3D interaction but trains only a segmentation head without a domain discriminator, performs no active selection, requires full supervision, and avoids pseudo-labeling. [D] It employs symmetrical 2D–3D attention to train a domain discriminator but selects only source frames, supports only semi-supervised settings, and improves UDA methods by data augmentation rather than pseudo-labeling.

source_questions.claim: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which configuration meets all of the following: symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction; cross-modal representations used to train a domain discriminator; domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames; effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings; and enhancement of existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data? [A] Symmetrical 2D–3D attention with cross-modal representations trains a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, supports unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings, and enhances UDA via targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data. [B] Asymmetrical 2D–3D fusion without a domain discriminator uses random frame sampling, supports only unsupervised settings, and pseudo-labels low-scoring target data. [C] 3D-only features train a class classifier with entropy-based passive selection, support only supervised settings, and avoid pseudo-labeling to prevent noise. [D] Symmetrical 2D–3D attention produces cross-modal features for a discriminator, but selection is limited to source frames, settings are semi-supervised only, and enhancement relies on augmenting labeled source data instead of pseudo-labeling.

source_questions.claim: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.
source_questions.question: Assertion (A): A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data. Reason (R): Domainness-driven active selection focuses training on informative frames across domains while targeted pseudo-labeling leverages high-scoring target data, leading to stronger adaptation.
source_questions.answer: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

source_questions.claim: A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data.
source_questions.question: Assertion (A): A unified domain adaptive 3D semantic segmentation approach uses symmetrical attention-based 2D–3D feature interaction to produce cross-modal representations that train a domain discriminator for domainness-driven active selection of informative source and target frames, enabling effective adaptation under unsupervised, few-shot unsupervised, and active annotation settings and enhancing existing UDA methods through targeted pseudo-labeling of high-scoring target data. Reason (R): The approach relies on random frame sampling and avoids pseudo-labeling to prevent bias.

paper_title: Predicting Companies' ESG Ratings from News Articles Using Multivariate Timeseries Analysis
paper_claims: Annual corporate ESG ratings can be inferred from news by modeling monthly multivariate timeseries that integrate weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios for relevant and irrelevant articles, and semantic topic cluster frequencies.

Formulating ESG rating prediction as a regression task over these timeseries with deep convolutional architectures yields superior predictive performance compared to classification and to index-aggregation baselines based on document-level transformer outputs.

Weak supervision using sentence-embedding similarity to ESG definitions effectively bootstraps a DistilBERT-based classifier to generate scalable ESG-relevance signals without manual keyword selection.

A large-scale news corpus aligned to thousands of US companies and annual third‑party ESG scores over multiple years is compiled and publicly released to support training and evaluation of ESG prediction models.

[A] Infer annual corporate ESG ratings from news by modeling monthly multivariate time series that integrate weakly supervised ESG-relevance detection, sentiment ratios for relevant and irrelevant articles, and semantic topic cluster frequencies. [B] Infer annual corporate ESG ratings from quarterly earnings by modeling quarterly univariate time series using only fully supervised ESG labels. [C] Infer annual corporate ESG ratings from social media by averaging daily sentiment without relevance filtering or topic clustering. [D] Infer annual corporate ESG ratings from news by aggregating annual raw article counts and applying unsupervised topic modeling alone.

paper_title: Semi-Supervised Learning of Monocular Depth Estimation via Consistency Regularization with K-way Disjoint Masking
paper_claims: A semi-supervised monocular depth estimation framework enforces uncertainty-weighted consistency between predictions from weakly and strongly augmented views generated via K-way disjoint masking, enabling effective learning from unlabeled images without stereo or video sequences.

K-way disjoint masking forms strong augmentations by independently encoding multiple disjoint token subsets and decoding their concatenation, which preserves global context, retains small objects, and mitigates scale ambiguity in depth prediction.

An auxiliary uncertainty estimation module weights pixelwise consistency to filter noisy pseudo-labels, while a predictor-headed feature consistency constraint stabilizes training and prevents collapse.
The approach remains robust under extremely sparse depth annotations and can be directly applied to unsupervised domain adaptation from synthetic to real data without additional generative models.

[A] It applies uncertainty-weighted consistency between predictions from weakly and strongly augmented views created via K-way disjoint masking to learn effectively from unlabeled images without stereo or video. [B] It relies on fully supervised losses with ground-truth depth and uses stereo or video sequences during training. [C] It imposes uniform consistency between identical augmentations produced with overlapping masks and depends on stereo sequences. [D] It uses uncertainty-weighted losses only on unaugmented views and trains exclusively on labeled images.

paper_title: Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries
paper_claims: Bayesian optimization with partially specified queries formalizes optimization where only a subset of input variables are specified and the remainder are randomly drawn, optimizing the conditional expectation over unspecified variables, and posterior-sampling algorithms for known and unknown input distributions—incorporating GP samples and UCB-style bonuses for empirical distribution estimates under an independence assumption—admit sublinear Bayesian cumulative regret for popular kernels without exponential dependence on dimensionality.

paper_title: Push-the-Boundary: Boundary-aware Feature Propagation for Semantic Segmentation of 3D Point Clouds
paper_claims: A boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, implemented in a multitask framework with a shared encoder that jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses the predicted boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and consequently reduces boundary-related segmentation errors.

source_questions.claim: A boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, implemented in a multitask framework with a shared encoder that jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses the predicted boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and consequently reduces boundary-related segmentation errors.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In 3D point cloud semantic segmentation, a boundary-aware feature propagation mechanism is implemented in a multitask framework with a shared encoder that jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels; it fuses the predicted boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries and consequently reduces boundary-related segmentation errors. Which option correctly reflects this description? [A] A multitask, shared-encoder design jointly predicts boundary locations, interior-pointing directions, and semantic labels; boundary and direction predictions are fused to propagate features toward true boundaries, reducing boundary-related segmentation errors. [B] A single-task design predicts only semantic labels without boundary or direction cues; features are propagated away from boundaries to reduce computation time. [C] Separate encoders in a multitask setup predict boundary locations and directions only; features are propagated toward object centers to reduce overall segmentation error. [D] A multitask, shared-encoder design predicts boundaries and labels but not directions; boundaries are used only for post-processing and do not affect feature propagation.

[C] Implemented as a multitask framework with a shared encoder, it jointly predicts object boundary locations, directions pointing toward object interiors, and semantic labels, fuses boundaries and directions to propagate features toward true boundaries, and reduces boundary-related segmentation errors. [D] Implemented for 2D image segmentation, it estimates semantic labels alone and applies a smoothing filter near edges to reduce boundary errors.

paper_title: Personalized Federated Recommender Systems with Private and Partially Federated AutoEncoders
paper_claims: A federated recommender architecture that trains client-specific encoders in an autoencoder locally while aggregating only a global decoder enables private personalized recommendations, improves convergence and accuracy relative to federated averaging and attains performance close to centralized training, while substantially reducing computation and communication by updating and transmitting only decoder parameters associated with rated items.

paper_title: Progressive Backdoor Erasing via connecting Backdoor and Adversarial Attacks
paper_claims: In a linear classifier with an additive patch trigger, the optimal untargeted adversarial perturbation has a substantial projection onto the trigger direction, theoretically explaining the similarity between adversarial and triggered inputs.

Fine-tuning an infected model on untargeted adversarial examples associates trigger-like patterns with multiple labels and thereby disrupts the trigger–target correlation that drives backdoor behavior while preserving performance on clean data.

Backdoors can be erased without a clean extra dataset by progressively alternating adversarial fine-tuning with clean-sample identification based on logit-similarity between infected and purified models to iteratively refine both the model and the auxiliary dataset.

[C] In a linear classifier with a multiplicative trigger, the optimal untargeted perturbation avoids the trigger direction, explaining dissimilarity. [D] In any classifier without triggers, optimal untargeted perturbations align with arbitrary directions, explaining similarity.

paper_title: Sensing-Throughput Tradeoffs with Generative Adversarial Networks for NextG Spectrum Sharing
paper_claims: In NextG spectrum sharing with periodic retraining over channel coherence frames, augmenting limited real spectrum sensing data with GAN-generated synthetic samples enables shorter sensing phases while maintaining low false alarm and misdetection rates, thereby increasing user throughput and protecting incumbents under both AWGN and Rayleigh fading.

The benefit of GAN-based sensing data augmentation is greater under Rayleigh fading than AWGN due to higher channel variability, but excessive reliance on synthetic samples can degrade classifier accuracy, implying an optimal balance between real and generated training data.

paper_title: Foresight - Deep Generative Modelling of Patient Timelines using Electronic Health Records
paper_claims: A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded patient timelines derived from both free-text and structured EHR data can forecast multi-step future biomedical concepts (disorders, substances, procedures, findings) across hospitals, yielding clinically relevant predictions and performing best for recurring events when using daily temporal resolution.

[C] A generative autoregressive transformer trained on SNOMED-coded timelines from free-text alone forecasts multi-step future lab measurements across hospitals, but shows the strongest performance for isolated events at monthly resolution without clear clinical relevance. [D] A generative encoder-only transformer trained on ICD-coded claims data forecasts only next-visit diagnoses in a single institution and performs best at hourly resolution for nonrecurring events, providing primarily research-oriented predictions.

paper_title: Difformer: Empowering Diffusion Model on Embedding Space for Text Generation
paper_claims: In embedding diffusion models for text, training with the standard denoising objective causes collapse of the learnable embedding space, producing anisotropic and poorly distinguishable token representations and destabilizing optimization.

An anchor loss that maximizes token likelihood given the denoiser’s predicted clean embeddings enforces well-distributed and distinguishable embeddings while stabilizing training by directly coupling denoiser outputs to discrete targets.

In high-dimensional embedding spaces, conventional noise schedules provide insufficient corruption that drives the denoising model to a condition-agnostic nearest-neighbor predictor, with the degenerate solution becoming globally optimal as noise vanishes and dimension grows.

A noise rescaling framework guided by a degeneration score that quantifies the accuracy of the degenerate predictor increases schedule variance via a multiplicative factor to prevent degeneration, independently of the base noise schedule.

A Transformer-based embedding diffusion model equipped with explicit length prediction and two-dimensional parallel decoding over candidate lengths and initial noises enables fast inference via reverse-step subsampling while maintaining high generation quality and diversity.

paper_title: A model of checkpoint behavior for applications that have I/O
paper_claims: For MPI-based parallel applications that perform file I/O, the checkpoint file size is governed by workload, process and node counts, and collective I/O configuration (number of aggregators and aggregator buffer size), because the checkpoint image comprises application data, libraries, communication buffers, and an additional I/O buffer zone concentrated in aggregator processes whose magnitude depends on the MPI-IO implementation and its two-phase optimization.

paper_title: Deep-Learning-Assisted IoT-Based RIS for Cooperative Communications
paper_claims: Training deep neural networks on channel state information to compute RIS element phase shifts and to classify received symbols enables IoT-coordinated, low-complexity multi-RIS cooperative communications without a direct path, with reliability governed by relay placement under Rayleigh fading and path loss.

paper_title: Unsupervised Dense Retrieval Deserves Better Positive Pairs: Scalable Augmentation with Query Extraction and Generation
paper_claims: Unsupervised dense retrieval can be trained at scale by generating pseudo queries from documents via salient-span extraction and transferred summarization/keyword generation, yielding models that perform competitively across heterogeneous search tasks without human-annotated query–document pairs.

Using pretrained text generators to produce short topics or titles transfers beneficial inductive bias for retriever pretraining and is more effective than heuristic random cropping as a source of pseudo queries.

Momentum-encoder contrastive training is more robust to noisy pseudo queries and benefits from mixing random crops with higher-quality pseudo queries, whereas in-batch contrastive training depends more on higher-quality pseudo queries and gains less from such mixing.
In-domain pseudo topic generation for target collections enables effective unsupervised domain adaptation of dense retrievers without supervised queries, hard negatives, or rerankers.

paper_title: Explainable depression detection with multi-aspect features using a hybrid deep learning model on social media
paper_claims: Integrating hierarchical attention-based representations of user tweets with multi-aspect behavioral features—including social interactions, posting-time patterns, emoji sentiment with valence–arousal–dominance signals, topic distributions, and domain-specific symptom and medication cues—yields superior and explainable depression detection on social media compared with using textual or behavioral signals alone.

[B] Using only hierarchical attention-based tweet representations without behavioral features yields equivalent but less explainable depression detection compared with integrated approaches. [C] Integrating tweet representations with behavioral features degrades performance and reduces interpretability relative to text-only models. [D] Combining tweets with generic behavioral features while excluding emoji valence–arousal–dominance and symptom/medication cues yields the best and explainable performance over any combination.

paper_title: Real-time face alignment: evaluation methods, training strategies and implementation optimization
paper_claims: The laMSD, NJS-MSE, and NFDS-MSE metrics quantify inter-frame jitter and sensitivity to face detector initialization in face landmark tracking, enabling robustness assessment beyond standard point error metrics.

General Training eXtensions (GTX) that include geometric and photometric data augmentation, statistical normalization and noise injection, initialization variation, and teacher–student domain-specific annotation improve accuracy, robustness to illumination and extreme pose, and smoothness in tracking across different face alignment algorithms and devices.

Multicore-parallel C++ implementations and software optimization of cascaded regression face alignment algorithms provide real-time inference on embedded and desktop platforms without degrading landmark localization accuracy.
Leveraging temporal information through filtering converts still-image face landmark detectors into stable real-time trackers that reduce jitter and failures under rapid head pose changes.

paper_title: Deep Learning Based Customer Preferences Analysis in Industry 4.0 Environment
paper_claims: A deep learning approach that partitions restaurant sales time series by day-of-week and holiday type, models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days effectively captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand while remaining computationally lightweight for real-time deployment.

source_questions.claim: A deep learning approach that partitions restaurant sales time series by day-of-week and holiday type, models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days effectively captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand while remaining computationally lightweight for real-time deployment.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In restaurant sales time series, which approach partitions data by day-of-week and holiday type, models ordinary days with stacked LSTMs, and uses MLPs to infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate it across holiday days, thereby capturing weekly seasonality and event-driven surges in nonlinear, intermittent demand while remaining computationally lightweight for real-time deployment? [A] Partition by day-of-week and holiday type; stacked LSTMs for ordinary days; MLPs infer total holiday demand from pre-holiday signals and allocate across holiday days; lightweight for real-time and captures weekly seasonality and event-driven surges [B] Partition by week-of-month and promotion type; GRUs for all days; MLPs allocate post-holiday residuals; heavy computation unsuitable for real-time and weak seasonality capture [C] No partitioning; transformers handle all periods; MLPs model price effects; focuses on linear demand with limited weekly seasonality and high computational cost [D] Partition by day-of-week only; CNNs model ordinary days; rule-based holiday handling from pre- and post-holiday averages; moderate compute without reliable surge capture

paper_title: One-Stage Tree: end-to-end tree builder and pruner
paper_claims: A decision tree framework jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation while keeping paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintaining interpretability, reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivering improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees.

source_questions.claim: A decision tree framework jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation while keeping paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintaining interpretability, reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivering improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately characterizes a decision tree framework that jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation, keeps paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enables closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintains interpretability, reduces the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivers improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees? [A] It satisfies all of these properties simultaneously, including discrete paths via Gumbel-Softmax with proximal steps, closed-form leaves for MSE and cross-entropy, preserved interpretability, a reduced train–test gap, and improved performance and stability over CART and soft trees. [B] It uses continuous soft path relaxations without proximal updates, does not perform pruning, and neither yields closed-form leaf values nor improved performance over CART or soft trees. [C] It optimizes splits only in a single-level setup without pruning, relies on straight-through estimators for continuous paths, and lacks closed-form leaves under MSE or cross-entropy. [D] It enforces discrete paths but cannot provide closed-form leaf values for standard losses and shows no improvement in stability or predictive performance relative to CART and prior soft trees.

source_questions.claim: A decision tree framework jointly optimizes splits and pruning in a single end-to-end bilevel formulation while keeping paths and architecture discrete via Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization and proximal iterations, enabling closed-form leaf values under mean squared error and cross-entropy losses, maintaining interpretability, reducing the training–testing performance discrepancy, and delivering improved predictive performance and stability over CART and prior soft trees.

[A] A jointly optimized bilevel tree with discrete paths via Gumbel-Softmax and proximal steps and closed-form leaves for MSE and cross-entropy that outperforms CART and soft trees and narrows the train–test gap. [B] A soft tree trained with continuous relaxations and no pruning that matches CART in stability but lacks closed-form leaf solutions for standard losses. [C] A tree that prunes post hoc after training splits, uses temperature-annealed softmax without proximal updates, and cannot guarantee interpretability or improved generalization. [D] A single-level optimization of splits only with discrete constraints that does not improve over CART and does not reduce the train–test discrepancy.

paper_title: Online Support Vector Regression Based Adaptive NARMA-L2 Controller for Nonlinear Systems
paper_claims: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which description correctly states how an adaptive NARMA-L2 controller identifies F and G, computes the control input u[n], updates its models, and what properties this confers regarding local minima and real-time adaptation to nonlinear changes? [A] It identifies F and G online from streaming input-output data using support vector regression, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors via convex, globally optimal margin-based adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation. [B] It identifies only F using batch least squares from stored data, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))*G_hat(X_n), and updates parameters by gradient descent that can be trapped in local minima, limiting real-time adaptation. [C] It tracks only G using an online Kalman filter, computes u[n]=r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)+G_hat(X_n), and employs nonconvex, marginless updates that provide no global optimality or real-time adaptation guarantees. [D] It uses offline SVR with fixed models, computes u[n]=(r[n]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates a single regressor with nonconvex training prone to local minima and slow adaptation.

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Select the statement that matches the full behavior of an adaptive NARMA-L2 controller regarding online identification of F and G, the exact control law, the nature of the learning updates, and the resulting avoidance of local minima and real-time adaptation. [A] It identifies subdynamics with stochastic gradient backpropagation, uses u[n]=(r[n]-F_hat(X_n))*G_hat(X_n), and may get trapped in local minima, making real-time adaptation difficult. [B] It uses online support vector regression on streaming input-output data to directly identify F and G, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments that avoid local minima of gradient-based identification and enable real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes. [C] It employs recursive least squares to estimate a linearized F while keeping G constant, uses u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)), and relies on nonconvex updates without margin guarantees, limiting adaptation. [D] It trains a single SVR offline to approximate F+G and computes u[n]=G_hat(X_n)/(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n)), which does not avoid local minima or support real-time adaptation.

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller directly identifies subdynamics F and G from streaming input-output data via online support vector regression, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments that avoid local minima of gradient-based identification and enable real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller identifies F and G online using support vector regression from streaming input-output data but computes u[n]=(r[n]-F_hat(X_n))*G_hat(X_n) and updates its regressors via nonconvex gradient-based descent, so it does not avoid local minima or enable real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: Assertion (A): An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller directly identifies F and G from streaming input-output data via online support vector regression, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and updates two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes. Reason (R): Online SVR solves a convex margin-based optimization for F and G that yields globally optimal updates from streaming data, which avoid local minima and support the NARMA-L2 control law’s real-time adaptation.
source_questions.answer: A is True, R is True, and R explains A

source_questions.claim: An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller that directly identifies its subdynamics F and G via online support vector regression from streaming input–output data computes control actions using u[n]=(r[n+1]−F̂(Xn))/Ĝ(Xn) while updating two SVR regressors through convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter adjustments, thereby avoiding local minima of gradient-based identification and enabling real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes.
source_questions.question: Assertion (A): An adaptive NARMA-L2 controller identifies F and G online from streaming input-output data using support vector regression, computes u[n]=(r[n+1]-F_hat(X_n))/G_hat(X_n), and uses convex, globally optimal margin-based parameter updates that avoid local minima and enable real-time adaptation to nonlinear system changes. Reason (R): This is because the control input multiplies by G_hat(X_n) and parameters are tuned by nonconvex gradient descent, which inherently avoids local minima.
source_questions.answer: A is True, R is False

paper_title: Enhancing spatial perception through sound: mapping human movements into MIDI
paper_claims: A two-layer grid that maps tracked body positions in video frames to MIDI note and control-change values enables real-time, expressive sonification of movement which, augmented by analog Theremin-based proximity modulation and transcribed into scientific pitch notation for multi-clef music sheets, improves users’ spatial perception and adaptation in an unfamiliar dark-room environment.

[B] It performs offline, non-expressive audio rendering using a single-layer grid and digital Theremin emulation, produces guitar tablature, and shows no effect on spatial perception in well-lit spaces. [C] It maps video pixels to audio amplitudes without MIDI, omits proximity modulation, avoids notation, and primarily addresses auditory memory rather than spatial adaptation. [D] It triggers fixed sound samples from skeletal joints with no control-change data, relies on light-based feedback instead of Theremin, exports only waveform plots, and degrades adaptation in dark rooms.

[C] A two-layer grid maps tracked body positions to both MIDI note and control-change values to enable real-time, expressive sonification, augmented by analog Theremin-based proximity modulation, and the output is transcribed into scientific pitch notation for multi-clef sheets, leading to improved spatial perception and adaptation in an unfamiliar dark-room environment. paper_title: How to measure uncertainty in uncertainty sampling for active learning
paper_claims: Separating predictive uncertainty into reducible epistemic and irreducible aleatoric components enables uncertainty sampling rules that target epistemic uncertainty as the more informative criterion.

Feature-wise evidence-based uncertainty measures disregard class priors and model aggregation, do not capture model uncertainty, and can therefore behave inconsistently with predictive entropy under class imbalance and linear models.
Uncertainty sampling with Naïve Bayes induces sampling bias that distorts estimated class priors toward frequently queried uncertain instances and can yield suboptimal decisions.
Credal uncertainty derived from probability intervals correlates strongly with epistemic uncertainty and poorly with aleatoric uncertainty, effectively acting as a measure of epistemic uncertainty.

Weighting candidate models by relative likelihood provides normalized degrees of class support that decompose total uncertainty into separate epistemic and aleatoric parts and afford graded plausibility advantages over hard credal sets.

[B] Predictive uncertainty is not separated, and sampling should target total uncertainty regardless of type. [C] Predictive uncertainty is separated into reducible aleatoric and irreducible epistemic components, and sampling should target aleatoric uncertainty as more informative. [D] Predictive uncertainty is separated into epistemic and aleatoric parts, and sampling prioritizes aleatoric uncertainty because it reflects inherent noise that can be reduced.

paper_claims: A shallow convolutional neural network with progressively smaller convolution kernels and pooling followed by two fully connected layers effectively detects agglomerate fog in images by capturing multi-scale global and local features while reducing overfitting and computational cost relative to deeper architectures.

Partitioning an image into sub-images and aggregating sub-classification decisions via voting improves robustness of agglomerate fog detection compared with whole-image classification by mitigating the influence of fog-free regions.
Applying dropout to the fully connected layers enhances generalization performance for agglomerate fog detection compared to training without dropout.
Training with synthetic fog generated using the atmospheric scattering model produces data sufficient to learn detectors that transfer to real-world images.

paper_title: ReliefE: feature ranking in high-dimensional spaces via manifold embeddings
paper_claims: ReliefE performs feature ranking by computing neighbor relations in low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, for multi-label tasks, the label space, enabling scalable operation on high-dimensional data via sparse-matrix computation.

The intrinsic dimensionality of the embedding space is estimated from the empirical distribution of ratios between distances to the two nearest nonidentical neighbors, eliminating manual selection of embedding dimension.
The number of neighbors used in the update step is selected adaptively per instance by identifying the largest gap in the sorted distance distribution, obviating a fixed k hyperparameter.
Updating feature weights against the average representation of the selected neighbors improves robustness to outliers compared to averaging per-neighbor updates.
Probabilistic matrix sparsification and representative subspace training reduce memory and time costs of embedding while maintaining the quality of the resulting feature rankings.

source_questions.claim: ReliefE performs feature ranking by computing neighbor relations in low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, for multi-label tasks, the label space, enabling scalable operation on high-dimensional data via sparse-matrix computation.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which option accurately describes ReliefE's overall approach to feature ranking and scalability across high-dimensional, including multi-label, datasets? [A] It ranks features by computing neighbor relations in low-dimensional manifold embeddings of the instance space and, for multi-label tasks, the label space, enabling scalable operation on high-dimensional data via sparse-matrix computation. [B] It ranks features by measuring global feature–label correlations in the original high-dimensional space and scales by using dense-matrix computation. paper_title: RoleSim*: Scaling axiomatic role-based similarity ranking on large graphs
paper_claims: RoleSim* defines role-based similarity as a convex combination of average similarities over maximum bipartite matchings of in-neighbors and over the remaining in-neighbor pairs, thereby preserving automorphic equivalence while incorporating non-matching neighbor information.
The difference between the k-th iterative RoleSim* score and its exact value is bounded by an exponentially decaying term in β, enabling a priori iteration bounds for a desired accuracy.
Threshold-based pruning and single-source retrieval variants of RoleSim* reduce computational time and memory while providing provable accuracy guarantees and scalability on large graphs.

[D] It selects the maximum single-pair similarity among in-neighbors and sets others to zero to preserve structural equivalence but not automorphic equivalence.

paper_title: pfd-parallel, a Singular/GPI-Space package for massively parallel multivariate partial fractioning
paper_claims: A modular, open-source framework for multivariate partial fraction decomposition combines an improved Leinartas method—with a preliminary numerator division and an algebraic-dependence step using annihilating polynomials chosen to ensure termination under graded reverse lexicographic order—with the MultivariateApart algorithm, and realizes the computation as Petri-net workflows in GPI-Space/Singular that provide intertwined parallelism across coefficients and within termwise decompositions via fork/join with early cancellations and size-aware scheduling, producing sums whose denominators are algebraically independent with common zeros and whose numerators are reduced with respect to the denominator ideal.

paper_title: ModelPred: A Framework for Predicting Trained Model from Training Data
paper_claims: A permutation-invariant neural network that ingests the contents of a training subset can predict the parameters of the model produced by a specified learning algorithm on that subset, generalizing to subsets with unseen points and enabling downstream tasks such as data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration.

Augmenting the predictor’s objective with a global utility-consistency regularizer and a local Karush–Kuhn–Tucker optimality regularizer constrains predicted models to be high-utility and near-stationary, thereby mitigating overfitting.

For learning problems with strongly convex or smooth objectives and bounded derivatives, the dataset-to-parameters mapping has bounded sensitivity and is efficiently approximable by ReLU neural networks.

[A] It takes a training subset as an unordered set to predict the specified learning algorithm’s resulting model parameters for that subset, generalizes to subsets with unseen points, and supports downstream tasks including data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, and prediction calibration. [B] It processes a fixed-order sequence of the entire training set to output labels for test points, cannot generalize to subsets with unseen points, and is not intended for tasks like data valuation, data selection, memorization assessment, or prediction calibration. [C] It ingests a training subset but predicts only hyperparameters of an unspecified algorithm; any generalization to new subsets is not supported, and the primary downstream use is hyperparameter tuning. [D] It is permutation-invariant and predicts model parameters, but only when all points were seen during training; downstream tasks are limited to model compression.

[D] A network that predicts hyperparameters for any learning algorithm but requires the subset’s examples to be in a fixed order and does not support tasks like data valuation or prediction calibration.

paper_title: A MECS redistribution algorithm for SDN-enable MEC using response time and transmission overhead
paper_claims: In SDN-enabled MEC with multiple controllers, modeling controller response time as M/G/1 sojourn plus hop-based propagation and transmission overhead as hop-weighted request load, and solving the resulting bi-objective MECS-to-controller assignment via a decomposition-based evolutionary algorithm with coordinate-transformation selection, jointly reduces control-plane response time and data-plane PACKET_IN transmission overhead while balancing controller load under capacity and limited-migration constraints.

Capping the maximum number of concurrent MECS redistributions per step reduces search space and prevents transient controller resource spikes, enabling convergence to high-quality assignments within effective time.

Increasing the number of controllers lowers average response delay and transmission overhead only up to a topology- and load-dependent saturation point, after which additional controllers provide no benefit and may degrade performance in heterogeneous (scale-free) networks if topology is not considered.

paper_title: MATTER: A tool for generating end-to-end IoT test scripts
paper_claims: A model-based approach uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible with a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimising redundancy in the resulting suite.

The produced test scripts exercise the system through its user interface via standard automation frameworks while stimulating and observing sensors and actuators through virtualized components or adapters for physical devices.
Arbitrary environmental inputs are incorporated by equivalence partitioning, encoding each partition as distinct operations in the model to enable automated path generation.

On representative IoT systems evaluated with mutation testing, the generated test suites effectively detect faults, and the suite minimisation step reduces execution time without compromising fault detection capability.

source_questions.claim: A model-based approach uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible with a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimising redundancy in the resulting suite.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): Which statement accurately characterizes a model-based approach for IoT systems that uses UML artifacts to produce executable end-to-end tests, including its coverage criterion, executability strategy, and suite optimization? [A] It uses UML state machine and class diagrams of an IoT system to automatically derive executable end-to-end test scripts by generating all-transitions-covering test paths, making them feasible via a branch-distance-guided executability heuristic, and minimizing redundancy in the resulting suite. [B] It uses only UML state machine diagrams to manually write integration tests by sampling random paths, accepting infeasible paths, and eliminating redundancy only by removing identical scripts. [C] It uses sequence and component diagrams of any software to generate unit tests with all-states coverage and no executability guidance, retaining all generated tests. [D] It uses UML class diagrams of an IoT system to translate requirements into high-level test ideas, relying on constraint solving for path feasibility and maximizing test diversity rather than minimizing redundancy.

paper_title: CORL: Compositional Representation Learning for Few-Shot Classification
paper_claims: Few-shot image classification can be performed by representing objects as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns, learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization and modulated by an attention mechanism that reweights class-discriminative components, enabling efficient transfer to novel classes by recombining these reusable elements.
During meta-testing, unseen classes can be recognized by training only a simple classifier on the learned compositional features while keeping the backbone and dictionaries fixed.

source_questions.claim: Few-shot image classification can be performed by representing objects as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns, learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization and modulated by an attention mechanism that reweights class-discriminative components, enabling efficient transfer to novel classes by recombining these reusable elements.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In few-shot image classification, which configuration matches the following description: objects are represented as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns; these dictionaries are learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization; an attention mechanism reweights class-discriminative components; and efficient transfer to novel classes is achieved by recombining the reusable elements? [A] Use two shared dictionaries (component embeddings and prototypical component spatial activation patterns) learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity, plus attention that reweights discriminative components to enable recombination-based transfer. [B] Use per-class, non-shared component dictionaries trained without clustering or sparsity and without attention, with transfer achieved only by full fine-tuning rather than recombination. [C] Use a single dictionary of holistic image prototypes without spatial activation patterns and without attention, with transfer not based on recombining reusable elements. [D] Use two shared dictionaries learned in separate stages without clustering or sparsity, no attention reweighting, and transfer that does not rely on recombination.

source_questions.claim: Few-shot image classification can be performed by representing objects as compositions of a shared dictionary of component embeddings and a shared dictionary of prototypical component spatial activation patterns, learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity regularization and modulated by an attention mechanism that reweights class-discriminative components, enabling efficient transfer to novel classes by recombining these reusable elements.

[A] Class-specific templates trained without clustering or sparsity, with attention suppressing discriminative components and transfer via full fine-tuning. [B] A single shared dictionary of spatial activation patterns only, with attention reweighting background components, and transfer not achieved through recombination. [C] Two shared dictionaries—component embeddings and prototypical component spatial activation patterns—learned end-to-end with clustering and sparsity, with attention reweighting class-discriminative components to enable recombination-based transfer to novel classes. [D] Many-shot image classification using per-class embeddings not learned end-to-end, without clustering or sparsity and without attention, with transfer requiring retraining rather than recombination.

paper_title: Event Distortion-Based Clustering Algorithm for Energy Harvesting Wireless Sensor Networks
paper_claims: Distortion-aware K-means clustering in RF energy-harvesting wireless sensor networks with spatially correlated observations, using MMSE-based single-hop and two-hop distortion models, selects only essential reporting nodes and designates the rest as relay heads while meeting a target reconstruction distortion.

Two-hop clustered relaying reduces energy consumption and extends network lifetime compared with single-hop direct transmission, at the cost of higher reconstruction distortion arising from the additional relay channel noise.

Exploiting ambient RF energy harvesting within the distortion-aware clustering framework increases the number of alive nodes and prolongs operation duration as harvested power increases without violating distortion constraints.

source_questions.claim: Distortion-aware K-means clustering in RF energy-harvesting wireless sensor networks with spatially correlated observations, using MMSE-based single-hop and two-hop distortion models, selects only essential reporting nodes and designates the rest as relay heads while meeting a target reconstruction distortion.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In RF energy-harvesting wireless sensor networks with spatially correlated observations, what does distortion-aware K-means clustering do with respect to distortion modeling, node-role assignment, and reconstruction quality? [A] It uses MMSE-based single-hop and two-hop distortion models to select only essential reporting nodes and assign the remaining nodes as relay heads while satisfying a target reconstruction distortion. [B] It ignores distortion and uses only geometric distances to select all nodes as direct reporters with no relays and no distortion target. [C] It relies on throughput-based multi-hop delay models to pick one cluster head per cluster and put all other nodes into sleep mode without considering reconstruction distortion. [D] It applies only a single-hop MMSE model, assigns non-essential nodes to sleep, and does not enforce a reconstruction distortion target.

[A] It operates in networks with independent observations and uses MAP-based multi-hop models to make all nodes relays, ignoring reconstruction distortion. [B] It targets battery-powered networks and uses only single-hop MMSE to make every node a reporting node without role differentiation or distortion guarantees. [C] It operates in RF energy-harvesting networks with spatially correlated observations by using MMSE-based single-hop and two-hop distortion models to select only essential reporting nodes and designate the remaining nodes as relay heads so that a specified reconstruction distortion is met. [D] It uses standard K-means without distortion awareness to randomly alternate node roles for energy balancing, with no target on reconstruction distortion.

paper_title: A machine learning based sentient multimedia framework to increase safety at work
paper_claims: An integrated sentient multimedia architecture that combines edge machine-learning wearable personal devices, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform enables real-time workplace safety by fusing multi-source sensor data, classifying hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issuing coordinated alarms and guidance through predictive and prescriptive analytics.

source_questions.claim: An integrated sentient multimedia architecture that combines edge machine-learning wearable personal devices, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform enables real-time workplace safety by fusing multi-source sensor data, classifying hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issuing coordinated alarms and guidance through predictive and prescriptive analytics.

[A] An integrated architecture that combines edge ML wearables and fixed area devices with a cloud safety coordination platform, fuses heterogeneous sensor streams, detects hazardous events (such as falls) from accelerometer and gyroscope data via low-pass filtering and statistical features, and delivers coordinated alarms and guidance using predictive and prescriptive analytics in real time. [B] A cloud-only setup that analyzes a single sensor stream with high-pass filtering, flags general activities offline, and sends uncoordinated notifications without guidance. [C] A wearable-only approach without fixed area devices or cloud coordination that reads only accelerometer data, thresholds raw signals without features, and generates delayed reports. [D] An integrated device network that provides descriptive dashboards, emphasizes routine activity classification instead of hazardous event detection, and issues independent device alerts without coordinated guidance.

source_questions.claim: An integrated sentient multimedia architecture that combines edge machine-learning wearable personal devices, fixed area devices, and a cloud-based safety coordination platform enables real-time workplace safety by fusing multi-source sensor data, classifying hazardous events such as falls from accelerometer and gyroscope signals using low-pass filtering with statistical features, and issuing coordinated alarms and guidance through predictive and prescriptive analytics.

[A] Edge wearables plus fixed area devices with cloud coordination, multi-sensor fusion, low-pass filtering with statistical features on accelerometer and gyroscope data to classify hazardous events, and predictive and prescriptive analytics for coordinated alarms and guidance in real time. [B] Edge wearables with cloud support only, independent per-sensor processing using band-pass filtering without statistical features, and uncoordinated alerts without prescriptive analytics. [C] Fixed area devices without edge wearables, high-pass filtering with heuristic rules to identify general motion patterns, and periodic summaries instead of real-time guidance. [D] All three tiers present but no data fusion, omission of hazardous event classification, and exclusive use of descriptive analytics to provide dashboards without coordinated alarms.

paper_title: Social Influence Under Uncertainty in Interaction with Peers, Robots and Computers
paper_claims: In perceptual decision-making without accuracy feedback, prior beliefs about an agent’s nature bias reliance on its judgments, with social robots initially exerting stronger influence than humans or computers despite identical estimate distributions, and this bias weakens with repeated exposure to the partner’s responses.

In the absence of overt socially relevant signals, susceptibility to influence in human–human interaction is shaped by social normative dynamics beyond perceived competence, whereas susceptibility in human–robot interaction is governed primarily by informational assessments of the robot’s competence.

paper_title: Image classification-based brain tumour tissue segmentation
paper_claims: Combining CNN-derived features from a transfer-learned CIFAR architecture with a per-voxel histogram-based texture descriptor that encodes local neighborhood dependencies and normalized intensities, followed by decision tree classification, improves voxel-wise brain tumor tissue segmentation accuracy and boundary delineation in multi-modal MRI relative to using CNN features alone while maintaining low model complexity.

source_questions.claim: Combining CNN-derived features from a transfer-learned CIFAR architecture with a per-voxel histogram-based texture descriptor that encodes local neighborhood dependencies and normalized intensities, followed by decision tree classification, improves voxel-wise brain tumor tissue segmentation accuracy and boundary delineation in multi-modal MRI relative to using CNN features alone while maintaining low model complexity.

[A] The combination of transfer-learned CIFAR CNN features with a per-voxel histogram-based texture descriptor encoding local neighborhood dependencies and normalized intensities, classified by a decision tree, improves voxel-wise brain tumor tissue segmentation accuracy and boundary delineation in multi-modal MRI relative to using CNN features alone and maintains low model complexity. [B] Using only CNN features from a transfer-learned CIFAR architecture with a decision tree yields higher accuracy and sharper boundaries than combining them with any texture descriptor, albeit with increased model complexity. [C] Combining CNN features with a global intensity histogram descriptor and classifying with a support vector machine reduces segmentation accuracy and boundary precision in multi-modal MRI but simplifies the model. [D] Augmenting CNN features with a per-voxel histogram descriptor improves performance only in single-modal MRI and requires a deep ensemble, increasing model complexity.

paper_title: Analysis of the evolution and collaboration networks of citizen science scientific publications
paper_claims: Citizen science publications indexed in Web of Science have expanded rapidly across disciplines, and their co‑authorship network is highly modular and clustered with a relatively small connected core, indicating many medium‑sized, largely country‑homogeneous communities linked by a few central cross‑cutting researchers.

paper_title: Cited text span identification for scientific summarisation using pre-trained encoders
paper_claims: Fine-tuning pre-trained transformer encoders on in-domain unlabelled text or augmented citing–cited sentence pairs improves cited text span identification compared with using generic pre-trained encoders without domain adaptation.

Providing only the abstract and identified cited text spans of a reference article as input to a BERT-based extractive summariser can match or surpass the summary quality obtained from the full text while reducing input length.

[A] Performance improves under either of these domain-adaptive fine-tuning regimes [B] Performance remains unchanged because only supervised labeled data can help [C] Performance degrades due to overfitting to unlabeled or augmented data [D] There is improvement only when using generic encoders with no adaptation

paper_title: Hierarchical sparse Cholesky decomposition with applications to high-dimensional spatio-temporal filtering
paper_claims: A hierarchical Vecchia approximation defined by conditioning each hierarchical group on its nested ancestors yields a Gaussian-process model whose covariance and precision matrices have Cholesky factors that are both sparse, share the same sparsity pattern, and satisfy that the precision factor equals the inverse transpose of the covariance factor.

Under independent Gaussian observation noise, the posterior covariance and precision Cholesky factors induced by the hierarchical Vecchia prior retain exactly the same sparsity patterns as the prior factors.

The covariance Cholesky factor implied by the hierarchical Vecchia approximation equals the incomplete Cholesky factorization of the exact covariance restricted to the specified sparsity pattern, enabling inference that scales as O(n N^2) time and O(n N) memory when conditioning sets have size at most N.

Combining the hierarchical Vecchia structure with a Laplace approximation for exponential-family likelihoods yields a Newton–Raphson-based algorithm that exploits the same sparsity and converges under concavity to a Gaussian approximation at the posterior mode.

Embedding the hierarchical Vecchia structure into linear and linearized spatiotemporal state-space models produces Kalman–Vecchia–Laplace filters that preserve sparsity through forecast and update steps and scale approximately linearly with state dimension given sparse temporal evolution.

source_questions.claim: A hierarchical Vecchia approximation defined by conditioning each hierarchical group on its nested ancestors yields a Gaussian-process model whose covariance and precision matrices have Cholesky factors that are both sparse, share the same sparsity pattern, and satisfy that the precision factor equals the inverse transpose of the covariance factor.
source_questions.question: Choose the correct option (A–D): In a hierarchical Vecchia approximation defined by conditioning each hierarchical group on its nested ancestors, the induced Gaussian-process model has covariance and precision matrices whose Cholesky factors are characterized by which properties? [A] Both factors are sparse, share the same sparsity pattern, and the precision factor equals the inverse transpose of the covariance factor [B] Both factors are dense, have different sparsity patterns, and the precision factor equals the transpose of the covariance factor [C] Both factors are sparse but have different sparsity patterns, and the precision factor equals the covariance factor [D] The covariance factor is sparse while the precision factor is dense, and the precision factor equals the inverse (not inverse transpose) of the covariance factor

[A] Conditioning each hierarchical group on its nested ancestors [B] Conditioning each group on non-nested descendants [C] Conditioning each group on all other groups simultaneously [D] No conditioning between hierarchical groups

Reason (R): The nested-ancestor conditioning induces matching conditional independence structure, making the forward and inverse Cholesky factorizations share sparsity and be inverse transposes.
